maps.text: Map of Asia highlighting an approximate region that would have been governed by the Sui dynasty (隋代) (Sy, ). The area was marked approximately to provide a clear visualization for the reader.

common.text:
### Sui Dynasty (隋代)

When we talk about dynasties, whether in the West or the East, we generally associate them with a family ruling a region for generations. This fact leads us to an initial reflection.

Reflection 1: At least...

The Sui Dynasty (隋代) had only two (three) rulers during its existence (-), but in that short span of time, they accomplished a great deal.

To define a dynasty, what is more important: the number of rulers or the achievements of those people?

#### Together we are strong

One of the great achievements of the Sui Dynasty (隋代) was the unification of China after three centuries, a fact that allowed the development of art and culture, as well as administrative reforms (<htcite0>; <htcite1>; <htcite2>).

The period of unification was also marked by another important event in East Asia that, in addition to China (Zhongguo 中國), also occurred in Japan (Nihon koku 日本国): the establishment of Buddhism in these regions (<htcite3>; <htcite4>).

The presence of religion leads us to another reflection:

Reflection 2: Me? Why not us?

After years of disunity, China (Zhongguo 中國) was once again unified, but that is only one step. After that, there is the need to keep the nation united.

Among the great Buddhist teachings is the concept of “no-I”, which allows for a broader understanding of ourselves () and even of a nation.

Could Buddhist teachings have helped maintain the unification of the nation?

If, instead of that teaching, a vision based on the existence of enemies — an “other” as evil — had been adopted, would it have been more difficult to maintain unity?

history.text: This is a powerful family originating from northern China (Zhongguo 中國). It was a powerful family with the necessary prestige for the founder of the dynasty to marry a son of an emperor of the Northern Zhou dynasty (北周) (<htcite0>).

#### Early years and education

The description of his birth in the year <htdate0> helps us explain some facts related to the relationship between Buddhism and the Sui dynasty (隋代) (<htcite0>; <htcite1>):
• "...When Wendi (Yang Jian) was born in a convent [Zhixan], Taizu (Yang Zhong, father of Wendi) said, 'This son is protected by the Buddha of Heaven...'

Until the age of 13, he was raised by a Buddhist nun, after which he attended a school for nobles (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

By the age of 14, he began to rise rapidly in the service of the future rulers of northern China (Zhongguo 中國).(<htcite2>).

#### Rise to power

After the death of the Northern Zhou emperor at only 35 years old, doubts arose regarding his successor. With this, Yang Jian (楊堅), his wife, and his collaborators decided that he should assume the throne. After overcoming several revolts, he finally established the Sui dynasty (隋代) on <htdate0>, assuming the title of Emperor Wendi (隋文帝) (aka Wen or Wenti) (<htcite3>; <htcite4>; <htcite5>).

#### Reforms

After the unification of the northern kingdoms, there was a centralization and improvement of the central administration through a unified and simplified legal system. Furthermore, anti-corruption measures were adopted, with officials having a limited term of three or four years and being unable to govern in the region where they were born (<htcite0>).

To assist poorer farmers, the Equal Field System (*juntian* 均田) was reinforced, ensuring that small farmers would not lose their land to large landowners (<htcite1>; <htcite2>).

These legal system reforms perpetuated themselves, creating an entire legal foundation for China (Zhongguo 中國), which leads us to a new reflection:

Reflection 3: Stability and happiness

The system of laws we are studying served as the foundation for the organization of China (Zhongguo 中國) for over a millennium, demonstrating the importance of a legal system for the organization and prosperity of a nation ().

Does the stability of a legal system allow people to focus on other tasks?

Does this stability enable rulers to focus on the real needs of the people?

When laws are made not to improve life, but to maintain a system of government, does this tend to cause dissatisfaction?

source.page: The prosperity and legal system of ancient China

history.text:
#### Expansion of Buddhism

Even before assuming the position of emperor, Yang Jian (楊堅) allowed monks to reopen their monasteries, a fact that possibly helped him gain the sympathy of part of the population (<htcite0>).

Despite good relations with Buddhism, there were also disagreements with Buddhist (and Taoist) leaders, when he discovered that some leaders, influenced by Emperor Wen, had turned against him during his attempts to unify northern China (Zhongguo 中國) (<htcite1>).

Once the initial disagreements were overcome, Buddhism consolidated, leading to the construction of, for example, 2,846 monasteries and large statues, such as the one shown in **Figure 2**, during the government of Yang Jian (楊堅), and continuing to exert influence throughout the entire Tang dynasty (唐代) (<htcite2>; <htcite3>; <htcite4>).
Figure 2: Massive figure of Buddha Amitābha. © Trustees of the British Museum ( ).

#### End of life

In the year <htdate0>, taking advantage of internal problems among the Turks, the emperor managed to weaken their power in the regions of Turkestan and Mongolia. On the other hand, his attempts at conquest in the south were unsuccessful. Shortly afterward, he passed away (<htcite0>).
According to the consulted source, she was also known as Empress Wenxian (文獻皇后) (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).
According to the consulted source (<htcite0>).
According to the consulted sources (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

#### Family Life

Yang Guang (楊廣) was not the eldest son of the imperial couple Yang Jian (楊堅) and Dugu Qieluo (獨孤伽羅). Even so, due to attitudes considered better than those of his older brother, he gained his mother's support to consolidate himself as successor (<htcite0>).

Another important fact related to his life concerns his father's choice of his marriage to Empress Xiao (蕭后), a person from the south, a region where he lived until he assumed the position of crown prince in <htdate0> (<htcite1>).

In the year <htdate1>, Yang Guang (楊廣) (also known as Yangdi) assumed the throne, being one of those possibly responsible for his father's death (<htcite2>).

Reflection 4: On Power

We are faced with a scenario in which the son may have directly participated in his father's death and, as a consequence, became the next emperor. This fact brings to mind the phrases of Lord Acton ():

“Power tends to corrupt and absolute power corrupts absolutely. Great men are almost always bad men, even when they exercise influence and not authority; still more when you superadd the tendency of the certainty of corruption by authority.”

“Despotic power is always accompanied by corruption of morality.”

Do you agree with these conclusions of Lord Acton?

Would they apply to the case we are studying?

Do you know of other cases that validate Lord Acton's proposition?

#### His Government

The unification of territories initiated by his father was completed during his years of government, with his domains reaching regions such as Annam and Champa in Vietnam (<htcite0>).

Another highlight of his government was the major public works, such as the construction of the Grand Canal, which connected the Yellow and Yangtze Rivers, being about 40 meters (130 feet) wide. Furthermore, he rebuilt the Great Wall of China (**Figure 2**), separating China from Inner Asia (Zhongguo, 中國) (<htcite1>).
Figure 3: Great Wall of China. Photo taken by Giovanni Boccardi and made available on the UNESCO website (). Shared under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 3.0 IGO license.

Reflection 5: Walls

Have you ever stopped to think that walls, besides keeping the unwanted out, can also isolate you inside?

Nowadays, much is said about “bubbles” that provide people with only one type of information. Can you see similarities between bubbles and walls? Could these “bubbles” be just a softer way of saying that people are being imprisoned?

Before this text, had you already studied anything about China (Zhongguo, 中國)? If not, do you understand that you were inside a wall (bubble)?

#### The End of Life

Despite the aforementioned advances, his government was not marked only by successes, including in military campaigns. He tried to conquer the region of present-day Korea between <htdate0> and <htdate1>, but was defeated in those attempts, including in one of them, in which he personally commanded his army. These defeats helped create revolts within the empire (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

This entire scenario created the conditions for one of his officials, Li Yuan (李淵), to begin organizing the future Tang dynasty (唐代) (<htcite2>; <htcite3>; <htcite4>).
According to the source consulted (<htcite0>).
According to the consulted source (<htcite0>).

Nunca tuvo la oportunidad de acceder al trono, pues falleció en el año <htdate0> (<htcite0>); sin embargo, su hijo llegó a tener ese honor, aunque no es reconocido como tal en todas las fuentes consultadas.

Además de su nombre Yang Zhao (楊昭), también es conocido como Yuande (<htcite1>).
La [posible] existencia de su hijo evidencia la necesidad de la existencia de esta persona (<htcite0>)
Debido a las divergencias de las fuentes investigadas, asumimos que él es su hijo (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

After the death of his [supposed] father, despite being very young, he became the immediate successor of Emperor Yang Guang (楊廣), as he already held the title of Prince of Yue (越王) (<htcite0>).

Despite this, one of his grandfather/father's officials, Li Yuan (李淵), declared himself emperor after the latter's death (<htcite1>; <htcite2>; <htcite3>; <htcite4>).

The disagreements ultimately led to an inevitable confrontation between the Sui dynasty (隋代) and the new dynasty that wished to emerge, the Tang dynasty (唐代).

As one of the first events, General Wang Shichong (王世充) made Yang Tong (楊侗) the new emperor, who assumed power under the name of Emperor Gong of Sui (隋恭帝), and the war's outcomes became inevitable. More than one battle took place between the army led by Wang Shichong (王世充) and the rebel army of Li Mi (李密), who ended up being defeated after several battles (<htcite5>; <htcite6>).

Yang Tong's (楊侗) reign was short, as the same general who brought him to power also deposed him, trying to create his own dynasty, the Zheng dynasty (鄭代), which came to an end in 621 when the Tang dynasty (唐代) finally ceased to be contested (<htcite7>; <htcite8>).

Reflection 6: The emperor is me! No, it's me!

Imagine that you were in this entire transition process of China (Zhongguo, 中國). How would you behave until a single emperor assumed power?

Even though we know that Li Yuan (李淵) and the Tang dynasty (唐代) emerged victorious, did you at any point prefer the continuation of the Sui dynasty (隋代)?

As shown in this text and in another from History Tracers, the Tang dynasty (唐代) inherited and continued the advances of the dynasty studied in this text (). Should future governments always take advantage of the positive legacies of previous governments?

What do you think of governments that try to erase old memories, even positive ones, to assert that their government is better?

exercise_v2.question: Was the Sui dynasty (隋代) responsible for unifying China (Zhongguo, 中國) and creating the necessary foundations for a period of prosperity?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: The final unification occurred during the reign of Yang Guang (楊廣), after a military expansion towards southern Vietnam.

exercise_v2.question: Was the Sui dynasty (隋代) a dynasty that lasted for centuries?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: The Sui dynasty (隋代) lasted only a few decades; however, it created the necessary foundations for the Tang dynasty (唐代) to bring new times of glory that lasted several centuries.
